        {
            "TableName": "audit-table",
            "Item": DynamoDBAuditLogger._create_item(  # pyright: ignore[reportPrivateUsage]
                "req-123",
                "audit message",
                timestamp.replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc),
            ),
            "ReturnValues": "NONE",
        }
    ]


def test_dynamodb_audit_logger_get_logs_queries_by_id():
    ts_ms = 1778164245000
    pages = [
        {
            "Items": [
                {
                    "id": {"S": "req-1"},
                    "timestamp": {"N": str(ts_ms)},
                    "message": {"S": "msg"},
                },
            ]
        }
    ]
    client = StubDynamoDBClient(query_pages=pages)
    logger = DynamoDBAuditLogger(
        table_name="audit-table",
        dynamodb=cast(DynamoDBClient, cast(object, client)),
    )

    results = logger.get_logs("req-1")

    assert len(results) == 1
    assert results[0].id == "req-1"
    assert results[0].message == "msg"
    expected_ts = datetime.fromtimestamp(ts_ms / 1000, tz=timezone.utc)
    assert results[0].timestamp == expected_ts

    assert client._paginator is not None  # pyright: ignore[reportPrivateUsage]
    last_kwargs = client._paginator._last_kwargs  # pyright: ignore[reportPrivateUsage]
    assert last_kwargs["KeyConditionExpression"] == "#id = :id"
    assert last_kwargs["ExpressionAttributeValues"] == {":id": {"S": "req-1"}}
    assert last_kwargs["ScanIndexForward"] is True


def test_dynamodb_audit_logger_get_logs_paginates():
    ts1 = 1778164245000
    ts2 = 1778164305000
    pages = [
        {
            "Items": [
                {
                    "id": {"S": "req-1"},
                    "timestamp": {"N": str(ts1)},
                    "message": {"S": "first"},
                }
            ]
        },
        {
            "Items": [
                {
                    "id": {"S": "req-1"},
                    "timestamp": {"N": str(ts2)},
                    "message": {"S": "second"},
                }
            ]
        },
    ]
    client = StubDynamoDBClient(query_pages=pages)
    logger = DynamoDBAuditLogger(
        table_name="audit-table",
        dynamodb=cast(DynamoDBClient, cast(object, client)),
    )

    results = logger.get_logs("req-1")

    assert [e.message for e in results] == ["first", "second"]


def test_dynamodb_audit_logger_get_logs_returns_empty_when_no_items():
    client = StubDynamoDBClient(query_pages=[{"Items": []}])
    logger = DynamoDBAuditLogger(
        table_name="audit-table",
        dynamodb=cast(DynamoDBClient, cast(object, client)),
    )

    assert logger.get_logs("req-1") == []


def test_dynamodb_audit_logger_get_logs_handles_page_without_items_key():
    client = StubDynamoDBClient(query_pages=[{}])
    logger = DynamoDBAuditLogger(
        table_name="audit-table",
        dynamodb=cast(DynamoDBClient, cast(object, client)),
    )

    assert logger.get_logs("req-1") == []


# --- AuditEntry ---


def test_audit_entry_to_json():
    ts = datetime(2026, 5, 7, 14, 30, 45, tzinfo=timezone.utc)
    entry = AuditEntry(id="req-1", timestamp=ts, message="hello")

    result = entry.to_json()

    assert result == {
        "id": "req-1",
        "timestamp": "2026-05-07T14:30:45+00:00",
        "message": "hello",
    }


# --- S3AuditLogReader ---


def _make_stub_s3(
    body: bytes | None = None, error_code: str | None = None
) -> MagicMock:
    """Return a minimal MagicMock-based stub for boto3 S3 client.

    Returned as MagicMock (not Any/S3Client) so call sites retain access to
    MagicMock helpers like ``assert_called_once_with``. The MagicMock attr
    accesses below are inherently Any-typed by the stdlib stubs.
    """
    stub: MagicMock = MagicMock()
    get_object = cast(MagicMock, stub.get_object)
    if error_code:
        get_object.side_effect = ClientError(
            {"Error": {"Code": error_code, "Message": ""}}, "GetObject"
        )
    elif body is not None:
        response_body: MagicMock = MagicMock()
        cast(MagicMock, response_body.read).return_value = body
        get_object.return_value = {"Body": response_body}
    return stub


def test_s3_reader_returns_entries_from_gzipped_jsonl():
    ts = "2026-05-07T14:30:45+00:00"
    data = _make_gzipped_jsonl(
        [
            {"id": "req-1", "timestamp": ts, "message": "first"},
            {
                "id": "req-1",
                "timestamp": "2026-05-07T15:00:00+00:00",
                "message": "second",
            },
        ]
    )
    s3 = _make_stub_s3(body=data)
    reader = S3AuditLogReader(
        bucket="my-bucket", prefix="audit", s3_client=cast(S3Client, s3)
    )

    results = reader.get_logs("req-1")

    assert len(results) == 2
    assert results[0].message == "first"
    assert results[1].message == "second"
    cast(MagicMock, s3.get_object).assert_called_once_with(
        Bucket="my-bucket", Key="audit/req-1.jsonl.gz"
    )


def test_s3_reader_uses_prefix_in_key():
    data = _make_gzipped_jsonl(
        [{"id": "x", "timestamp": "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00", "message": "m"}]
    )
    s3 = _make_stub_s3(body=data)
    reader = S3AuditLogReader(
        bucket="bucket",
        prefix="some/prefix",
        s3_client=cast(S3Client, s3),
    )

    _ = reader.get_logs("x")

    cast(MagicMock, s3.get_object).assert_called_once_with(
        Bucket="bucket", Key="some/prefix/x.jsonl.gz"
    )


def test_s3_reader_strips_trailing_slash_from_prefix():
    data = _make_gzipped_jsonl(
        [{"id": "x", "timestamp": "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00", "message": "m"}]
    )
    s3 = _make_stub_s3(body=data)
    reader = S3AuditLogReader(
        bucket="bucket",
        prefix="some/prefix/",
        s3_client=cast(S3Client, s3),
    )

    _ = reader.get_logs("x")

    cast(MagicMock, s3.get_object).assert_called_once_with(
        Bucket="bucket", Key="some/prefix/x.jsonl.gz"
    )


def test_s3_reader_empty_prefix_omits_slash():
    data = _make_gzipped_jsonl(
        [{"id": "x", "timestamp": "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00", "message": "m"}]
    )
    s3 = _make_stub_s3(body=data)
    reader = S3AuditLogReader(
        bucket="bucket", prefix="", s3_client=cast(S3Client, s3)
    )

    _ = reader.get_logs("x")

    cast(MagicMock, s3.get_object).assert_called_once_with(
        Bucket="bucket", Key="x.jsonl.gz"
    )


def test_s3_reader_returns_empty_on_no_such_key():
    s3 = _make_stub_s3(error_code="NoSuchKey")
    reader = S3AuditLogReader(
        bucket="bucket", prefix="pfx", s3_client=cast(S3Client, s3)
    )

    assert reader.get_logs("missing-id") == []


def test_s3_reader_returns_empty_on_no_such_bucket():
    s3 = _make_stub_s3(error_code="NoSuchBucket")
    reader = S3AuditLogReader(
        bucket="bucket", prefix="pfx", s3_client=cast(S3Client, s3)
    )

    assert reader.get_logs("any-id") == []


def test_s3_reader_propagates_other_client_errors():
    s3 = _make_stub_s3(error_code="AccessDenied")
    reader = S3AuditLogReader(
        bucket="bucket", prefix="pfx", s3_client=cast(S3Client, s3)
    )

    with pytest.raises(ClientError):
        _ = reader.get_logs("req-1")


def test_s3_reader_raises_on_malformed_json():
    bad_bytes = gzip.compress(
        b'{"id": "x", "timestamp": "2026-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"\n{bad line'
    )
    s3 = _make_stub_s3(body=bad_bytes)
    reader = S3AuditLogReader(
        bucket="bucket", prefix="pfx", s3_client=cast(S3Client, s3)
    )

    with pytest.raises(json.JSONDecodeError):
        _ = reader.get_logs("x")


# --- FallbackAuditLogReader ---


def _make_fallback_s3_mock(
    return_value: list[AuditEntry] | None = None,
) -> MagicMock:
    mock: MagicMock = MagicMock(spec=S3AuditLogReader)
    if return_value is not None:
        cast(MagicMock, mock.get_logs).return_value = return_value
    return mock


def test_fallback_reader_returns_primary_when_non_empty():
    ts = datetime(2026, 1, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)
    primary = InMemoryAuditLogger()
    primary.log("req-1", "primary entry", ts)

    fallback_s3 = _make_fallback_s3_mock()

    reader = FallbackAuditLogReader(
        primary=primary, fallback=cast(S3AuditLogReader, fallback_s3)
    )
    results = reader.get_logs("req-1")

    assert len(results) == 1
    assert results[0].message == "primary entry"
    cast(MagicMock, fallback_s3.get_logs).assert_not_called()


def test_fallback_reader_falls_back_to_s3_when_primary_empty():
    primary = InMemoryAuditLogger()
    ts = datetime(2026, 1, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)
    expected = [AuditEntry(id="req-1", timestamp=ts, message="s3 entry")]

    fallback_s3 = _make_fallback_s3_mock(return_value=expected)

    reader = FallbackAuditLogReader(
        primary=primary, fallback=cast(S3AuditLogReader, fallback_s3)
    )
    results = reader.get_logs("req-1")

    assert results == expected
    cast(MagicMock, fallback_s3.get_logs).assert_called_once_with("req-1")


def test_fallback_reader_returns_empty_when_both_empty():
    primary = InMemoryAuditLogger()
    fallback_s3 = _make_fallback_s3_mock(return_value=[])

    reader = FallbackAuditLogReader(
        primary=primary, fallback=cast(S3AuditLogReader, fallback_s3)
    )
    assert reader.get_logs("req-1") == []


# --- Factory functions ---


def test_get_audit_logger_returns_noop_logger_without_env(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    monkeypatch.delenv(DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE, raising=False)

    logger = get_audit_logger()

    assert isinstance(logger, NoOpAuditLogger)


def test_get_audit_logger_returns_dynamodb_logger_when_env_set(
    monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch,
):
    expected = DynamoDBAuditLogger(
        table_name="audit-table",
        dynamodb=cast(DynamoDBClient, cast(object, StubDynamoDBClient())),
    )

    def fake_get_dynamodb_audit_logger(table_name: str) -> DynamoDBAuditLogger:
        return stub_get_dynamodb_audit_logger(table_name, expected)

    monkeypatch.setenv(DYNAMO_DB_AUDIT_LOGGING_TABLE, "audit-table")
    monkeypatch.setattr(
        "dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging.get_dynamodb_audit_logger",
        fake_get_dynamodb_audit_logger,
    )

    logger = get_audit_logger()

    assert logger is expected


def test_get_dynamodb_audit_logger_builds_boto3_client(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    client = StubDynamoDBClient()

    def fake_boto3_client(service_name: str) -> StubDynamoDBClient:
        return stub_boto3_client(service_name, client)

    monkeypatch.setattr(
        "dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging.boto3.client",
        fake_boto3_client,
    )

    logger = get_dynamodb_audit_logger("audit-table")

    assert isinstance(logger, DynamoDBAuditLogger)
    assert logger.table_name == "audit-table"
    assert logger.dynamodb is client


def test_get_s3_audit_log_reader_builds_boto3_client(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    s3_stub: MagicMock = MagicMock()
    calls: list[str] = []

    def fake_boto3_client(service_name: str) -> MagicMock:
        calls.append(service_name)
        return s3_stub

    monkeypatch.setattr(
        "dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging.boto3.client", fake_boto3_client
    )

    reader = get_s3_audit_log_reader("my-bucket", "my-prefix")

    assert isinstance(reader, S3AuditLogReader)
    assert reader.bucket == "my-bucket"
    assert reader.prefix == "my-prefix"
    assert "s3" in calls


def test_get_audit_log_reader_raises_without_s3_bucket(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    monkeypatch.delenv(AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET, raising=False)
    monkeypatch.setenv(AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX, "pfx")

    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match=AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET):
        _ = get_audit_log_reader()


def test_get_audit_log_reader_uses_empty_prefix_by_default(monkeypatch: MonkeyPatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv(AUDIT_LOG_S3_BUCKET, "my-bucket")
    monkeypatch.delenv(AUDIT_LOG_S3_PREFIX, raising=False)

    def fake_boto3_client(_service_name: str) -> MagicMock:
        return MagicMock()

    def fake_get_audit_logger() -> NoOpAuditLogger:
        return NoOpAuditLogger()

    monkeypatch.setattr(
        "dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging.boto3.client",
        fake_boto3_client,
    )
    monkeypatch.setattr(
        "dns_de_rte_orchestrator.audit_logging.get_audit_logger",
        fake_get_audit_logger,
    )

    reader = get_audit_log_reader()

    assert isinstance(reader, FallbackAuditLogReader)
    assert reader.fallback.prefix == ""

content.content:
1	# GDPR Right to Erasure Orchestration Platform
2	
3	[![made-with-python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Made%20with-Python-1f425f.svg)](https://www.python.org/)
4	[![prek](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/j178/prek/master/docs/assets/badge-v0.json)](https://github.com/j178/prek)
5	[![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)
6	[![pipeline status](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/pipeline.svg)](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/commits/master)
7	![coverage](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/coverage.svg?job=test)
8	
9	This project was originally created to replace the
10	[Tines](https://www.tines.com/) based Right to Erasure Orchestrator.
11	
12	## Project Goals
13	
14	The primary goal of this project is to replace the existing Tines based
15	orchestrator for subject erasure requests in regards to the GDPR legislation.
16	
17	The main problems being solved are as followed:
18	
19	- The costs related to the use of Tines
20	- The use of Jira as a point of integration and state storage
21	
22	This project also moves the ownership of the orchestration into the Data
23	Engineering team.
24	
25	## Project Details
26	
27	This project is built using Python 3.13+ and uses
28	[uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) as a package manager and build tool.
29	
30	> [!note]
31	> If you need help setting up your development environment, please refer to
32	> [docs/setup-local-dev.md](/docs/setup-local-dev.md).
33	
34	## Development
35	
36	After cloning this repository you should run:
37	
38	```sh
39	uv sync --all-groups
40	uv run prek install
41	```
42	
43	This should create a virtual environment under `.venv` in the local directory,
44	download all dependencies, and install a Git pre-commit hook.
45	
46	Generally speaking, if you want to run a normal Python command you should
47	prefix it with `uv run`.
48	
49	### Testing
50	
51	This project uses `pytest` for unit testing. These should be run using the
52	following command:
53	
54	```sh
55	uv run pytest
56	```
57	
58	`pytest-cov` is used as an extension that can help you see how much coverage
59	the tests are actually giving. You can run this as follows:
60	
61	```sh
62	uv run pytest --cov=dns_de_rte_orchestrator
63	```
64	
65	There isn't a set test coverage percentage code should have, but key files
66	should generally have more coverage than others.
67	
68	### Structure
69	
70	The key entry points for AWS Lambda are:
71	
72	- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py`
73	- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py`
74	
75	The storage logic sits primarily in the `RequestRepository` parent class and its
76	implementations in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py`.
77	
78	The business logic sits in the `RequestProcessor` class in
79	`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py`.
80	
81	### Managing Dependencies
82	
83	`pip` commands should not be used to manage dependencies, instead you should
84	use commands such as:
85	
86	```sh
87	# To add a library dependency
88	uv add library
89	# To add a development dependency only
90	uv add --dev pytest
91	# To remove a dependency
92	uv remove library
93	```
94	
95	This should update the `uv.lock` file, but if in doubt you can run `uv lock`
96	to regenerate it.
97	
98	You can attempt to upgrade packages using the following `uv` command:
99	
100	```sh
101	uv lock --upgrade-package library
102	```
103	
104	## Deployment
105	
106	The code in this project is deployed as 2 separate AWS Lambdas using [AWS
107	SAM](https://aws.amazon.com/serverless/sam/).
108	
109	The versions of Python used during development, testing, and deployment should
110	all match as closely as possible.
111	
112	GitLab runners are used to execute tests and deployments. The configuration for
113	this is managed in `.gitlab-ci.yml`.
114	
115	### SAM
116	
117	AWS SAM uses a custom `Makefile` for building the package that gets uploaded to
118	AWS. This `Makefile` should not be invoked manually. The gist of it is:
119	
120	- It generates a `requirements.txt` with all the non-dev dependencies based on
121	  the `uv.lock` file
122	- It installs all these requirements into the artifact directory
123	- It copies the project `src/` code into the artifact directory
124	- It deletes the `requirements.txt` file
125	
126	The reason for the custom process is to reduce the size of the uploaded
127	artifact and to keep it replicable across different AWS SAM versions.
128	
129	> [!note]
130	> `sam local invoke` has problems when combined with the
131	> [ServerlessAccessManager](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/platform-as-a-service/serverless-applications/serverless-access-manager/)
132	> that the DSO team provide for us to use. You'll need to comment it out and
133	> any lines that reference it in order to get `sam local invoke` to work.
134	
135	To do a manual deployment you'll need to do the following:
136	
137	```sh
138	# First run the build process, this will execute the Makefile
139	sam build
140	# Next deploy
141	sam deploy \
142	  --config-file ../../samconfig.toml \
143	  --config-env dev \
144	  --template-file .aws-sam/build/template.yaml
145	```
146	
147	Note that you'll need to point at the `samconfig.toml` in the project root. By
148	default AWS SAM will try and search the same directory as the template file for
149	the `samconfig.toml`.
150	
151	## Code Quality Tools
152	
153	This project makes use of several tools to ensure consistent code quality.
154	
155	You can run these tools locally alongside `pytest` by running:
156	
157	```sh
158	./local-ci.sh
159	```
160	
161	### prek
162	
163	[prek](https://prek.j178.dev/) is a replacement for the
164	[pre-commit](https://pre-commit.com/) framework. Both allow you to run scripts
165	and checks before a Git commit is created.
166	
167	`prek` is primarily used to run the other code quality tools and ensure that
168	the repository remains in a high quality state.
169	
170	You will need to install the Git pre-commit hooks once after cloning the
171	repository by running:
172	
173	```sh
174	uv run prek install
175	```
176	
177	This should force `prek` to run before all local commits.
178	
179	You can also manually run `prek` by using:
180	
181	```sh
182	uv run prek
183	```
184	
185	The `prek` configuration is stored in `prek.toml`. It configures many
186	pre-commit checks and runs `ruff`, `basedpyright` and `black`.
187	
188	### ruff
189	
190	[ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/) is both a fast linter AND code formatter
191	for Python. This project primarily uses it to lint code.
192	
193	You can run it via:
194	
195	```sh
196	uv run ruff check
197	```
198	
199	This can prevent common Python mistakes.
200	
201	### basedpyright
202	
203	[basedpyright](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/) is a fork of `pyright`.
204	It works as a static type-checker for Python code.
205	
206	You should include type hints on as much code as possible to help it out and
207	manage programmer expectations. You should also avoid using deprecated types
208	along with the `Any` type when possible.
209	
210	You can manually run `basedpyright` using `uv`:
211	
212	```sh
213	uv run basedpyright
214	```
215	
216	Sometimes libraries may not include enough type information for `basedpyright`.
217	In these instances you can create type stubs. This process can be done by hand,
218	or you can try to use `basedpyright` to generate said stubs for you via a
219	command like:
220	
221	```sh
222	uv run basedpyright --createstub library
223	```
224	
225	These stubs would be placed in the `typings/` directory at the root of the
226	project.
227	
228	Sometimes type stubs may not be enough, in this case you can add [ignore
229	comments](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/configuration/comments/) to the
230	code.
231	
232	```py
233	# pyright: ignore
234	```
235	
236	You can also edit the `pyrightconfig.json` and add specific libraries to
237	`allowedUntypedLibraries` to allow a whole library to be untyped.
238	
239	#### boto3
240	
241	For `boto3` type stubs there is a tool called
242	[mypy_boto3_builder](https://youtype.github.io/mypy_boto3_builder/) which is
243	used to generate type stubs. This is due to the fact that the `boto3` library
244	is updated frequently, is large, and doesn't include good type information.
245	
246	These stubs can be generated by using `uvx` and `mypy-boto3-builder`, however a
247	script has been provided to keep these up to date called
248	`regen-boto3-type-hints.sh` that will do this for you in a consistent manner.
249	You should use this script when updating the `boto3` version in
250	`pyproject.toml` (and keep it pinned to a specific version) or when you want to
251	add type hints for more AWS services.
252	
253	The type information for `boto3` is stored in the `vendored/` directory.
254	
255	> [!note]
256	> The way Python `.whl` files work means that regenerating them will always
257	> result in a different file as they contain timestamps within them. And the
258	> way `uv.lock` files work is that they store a hash of the `.whl` file. This
259	> is why the `vendored/` file is stored in the repository.
260	
261	When you refer to these types, you should use `TYPE_CHECKING` to guard their
262	inclusion in your code and replace them with stub definitions. For example:
263	
264	```py
265	from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any
266	
267	if TYPE_CHECKING:
268	    from types_boto3_dynamodb.client import DynamoDBClient
269	    from types_boto3_dynamodb.type_defs import (
270	        AttributeValueTypeDef,
271	        UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef,
272	    )
273	else:
274	    DynamoDBClient = Any
275	    AttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
276	    UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
277	```
278

# GDPR Right to Erasure Orchestration Platform

[![made-with-python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Made%20with-Python-1f425f.svg)](https://www.python.org/)
[![prek](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/j178/prek/master/docs/assets/badge-v0.json)](https://github.com/j178/prek)
[![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)
[![pipeline status](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/pipeline.svg)](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/commits/master)
![coverage](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/coverage.svg?job=test)

This project was originally created to replace the
[Tines](https://www.tines.com/) based Right to Erasure Orchestrator.

## Project Goals

The primary goal of this project is to replace the existing Tines based
orchestrator for subject erasure requests in regards to the GDPR legislation.

The main problems being solved are as followed:

- The costs related to the use of Tines
- The use of Jira as a point of integration and state storage

This project also moves the ownership of the orchestration into the Data
Engineering team.

## Project Details

This project is built using Python 3.13+ and uses
[uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) as a package manager and build tool.

> [!note]
> If you need help setting up your development environment, please refer to
> [docs/setup-local-dev.md](/docs/setup-local-dev.md).

## Development

After cloning this repository you should run:

```sh
uv sync --all-groups
uv run prek install
```

This should create a virtual environment under `.venv` in the local directory,
download all dependencies, and install a Git pre-commit hook.

Generally speaking, if you want to run a normal Python command you should
prefix it with `uv run`.

### Testing

This project uses `pytest` for unit testing. These should be run using the
following command:

```sh
uv run pytest
```

`pytest-cov` is used as an extension that can help you see how much coverage
the tests are actually giving. You can run this as follows:

```sh
uv run pytest --cov=dns_de_rte_orchestrator
```

There isn't a set test coverage percentage code should have, but key files
should generally have more coverage than others.

### Structure

The key entry points for AWS Lambda are:

- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py`
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py`

The storage logic sits primarily in the `RequestRepository` parent class and its
implementations in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py`.

The business logic sits in the `RequestProcessor` class in
`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py`.

### Managing Dependencies

`pip` commands should not be used to manage dependencies, instead you should
use commands such as:

```sh
# To add a library dependency
uv add library
# To add a development dependency only
uv add --dev pytest
# To remove a dependency
uv remove library
```

This should update the `uv.lock` file, but if in doubt you can run `uv lock`
to regenerate it.

You can attempt to upgrade packages using the following `uv` command:

```sh
uv lock --upgrade-package library
```

## Deployment

The code in this project is deployed as 2 separate AWS Lambdas using [AWS
SAM](https://aws.amazon.com/serverless/sam/).

The versions of Python used during development, testing, and deployment should
all match as closely as possible.

GitLab runners are used to execute tests and deployments. The configuration for
this is managed in `.gitlab-ci.yml`.

### SAM

AWS SAM uses a custom `Makefile` for building the package that gets uploaded to
AWS. This `Makefile` should not be invoked manually. The gist of it is:

- It generates a `requirements.txt` with all the non-dev dependencies based on
  the `uv.lock` file
- It installs all these requirements into the artifact directory
- It copies the project `src/` code into the artifact directory
- It deletes the `requirements.txt` file

The reason for the custom process is to reduce the size of the uploaded
artifact and to keep it replicable across different AWS SAM versions.

> [!note]
> `sam local invoke` has problems when combined with the
> [ServerlessAccessManager](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/platform-as-a-service/serverless-applications/serverless-access-manager/)
> that the DSO team provide for us to use. You'll need to comment it out and
> any lines that reference it in order to get `sam local invoke` to work.

To do a manual deployment you'll need to do the following:

```sh
# First run the build process, this will execute the Makefile
sam build
# Next deploy
sam deploy \
  --config-file ../../samconfig.toml \
  --config-env dev \
  --template-file .aws-sam/build/template.yaml
```

Note that you'll need to point at the `samconfig.toml` in the project root. By
default AWS SAM will try and search the same directory as the template file for
the `samconfig.toml`.

## Code Quality Tools

This project makes use of several tools to ensure consistent code quality.

You can run these tools locally alongside `pytest` by running:

```sh
./local-ci.sh
```

### prek

[prek](https://prek.j178.dev/) is a replacement for the
[pre-commit](https://pre-commit.com/) framework. Both allow you to run scripts
and checks before a Git commit is created.

`prek` is primarily used to run the other code quality tools and ensure that
the repository remains in a high quality state.

You will need to install the Git pre-commit hooks once after cloning the
repository by running:

```sh
uv run prek install
```

This should force `prek` to run before all local commits.

You can also manually run `prek` by using:

```sh
uv run prek
```

The `prek` configuration is stored in `prek.toml`. It configures many
pre-commit checks and runs `ruff`, `basedpyright` and `black`.

### ruff

[ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/) is both a fast linter AND code formatter
for Python. This project primarily uses it to lint code.

You can run it via:

```sh
uv run ruff check
```

This can prevent common Python mistakes.

### basedpyright

[basedpyright](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/) is a fork of `pyright`.
It works as a static type-checker for Python code.

You should include type hints on as much code as possible to help it out and
manage programmer expectations. You should also avoid using deprecated types
along with the `Any` type when possible.

You can manually run `basedpyright` using `uv`:

```sh
uv run basedpyright
```

Sometimes libraries may not include enough type information for `basedpyright`.
In these instances you can create type stubs. This process can be done by hand,
or you can try to use `basedpyright` to generate said stubs for you via a
command like:

```sh
uv run basedpyright --createstub library
```

These stubs would be placed in the `typings/` directory at the root of the
project.

Sometimes type stubs may not be enough, in this case you can add [ignore
comments](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/configuration/comments/) to the
code.

```py
# pyright: ignore
```

You can also edit the `pyrightconfig.json` and add specific libraries to
`allowedUntypedLibraries` to allow a whole library to be untyped.

#### boto3

For `boto3` type stubs there is a tool called
[mypy_boto3_builder](https://youtype.github.io/mypy_boto3_builder/) which is
used to generate type stubs. This is due to the fact that the `boto3` library
is updated frequently, is large, and doesn't include good type information.

These stubs can be generated by using `uvx` and `mypy-boto3-builder`, however a
script has been provided to keep these up to date called
`regen-boto3-type-hints.sh` that will do this for you in a consistent manner.
You should use this script when updating the `boto3` version in
`pyproject.toml` (and keep it pinned to a specific version) or when you want to
add type hints for more AWS services.

The type information for `boto3` is stored in the `vendored/` directory.

> [!note]
> The way Python `.whl` files work means that regenerating them will always
> result in a different file as they contain timestamps within them. And the
> way `uv.lock` files work is that they store a hash of the `.whl` file. This
> is why the `vendored/` file is stored in the repository.

When you refer to these types, you should use `TYPE_CHECKING` to guard their
inclusion in your code and replace them with stub definitions. For example:

```py
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Any

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from types_boto3_dynamodb.client import DynamoDBClient
    from types_boto3_dynamodb.type_defs import (
        AttributeValueTypeDef,
        UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef,
    )
else:
    DynamoDBClient = Any
    AttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
    UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
```

This is because the types are not included when the Python code is packaged and
built for AWS SAM deployments.

### black

[black](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) is a code formatter for
Python.

You can run `black` directly on a specific file using:

```sh
uv run black path/to/file.py
```

`black` is used to ensure all Python code follows the same standard of
formatting.

## API

The orchestrator exposes a private REST API (deployed via AWS API Gateway and
restricted to the configured VPC endpoint). All requests must include the
`x-api-key` header.

### `GET /status/{id}`

Returns the audit log entries for an erasure request, ordered chronologically
(oldest first). Backed by `reporter_lambda`, which reads from DynamoDB and
falls back to the materialised S3 audit log.

```sh
curl -k -sS -G \
  'https://<api-id>.execute-api.<region>.amazonaws.com/<env>/status/<id>' \
  -H 'x-api-key: <api-key>'
```

A `200` response has the form:

```json
{
  "id": "<id>",
  "entries": [
    { "id": "<id>", "timestamp": "<iso-8601>", "message": "<text>" }
  ]
}
```

The endpoint returns `404` if no audit entries exist for the given id, and
`400` if the id path parameter is missing or blank.

- It copies the project `src/` code into the artifact directory
- It deletes the `requirements.txt` file

The reason for the custom process is to reduce the size of the uploaded
artifact and to keep it replicable across different AWS SAM versions.

> [!note]
> `sam local invoke` has problems when combined with the
> [ServerlessAccessManager](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/platform-as-a-service/serverless-applications/serverless-access-manager/)
> that the DSO team provide for us to use. You'll need to comment it out and
> any lines that reference it in order to get `sam local invoke` to work.

To do a manual deployment you'll need to do the following:

```sh
# First run the build process, this will execute the Makefile
sam build
# Next deploy
sam deploy \
  --config-file ../../samconfig.toml \
  --config-env dev \
  --template-file .aws-sam/build/template.yaml
```

Note that you'll need to point at the `samconfig.toml` in the project root. By
default AWS SAM will try and search the same directory as the template file for
the `samconfig.toml`.

## Code Quality Tools

This project makes use of several tools to ensure consistent code quality.

You can run these tools locally alongside `pytest` by running:

```sh
./local-ci.sh
```

### prek

[prek](https://prek.j178.dev/) is a replacement for the
[pre-commit](https://pre-commit.com/) framework. Both allow you to run scripts
and checks before a Git commit is created.

`prek` is primarily used to run the other code quality tools and ensure that
the repository remains in a high quality state.

You will need to install the Git pre-commit hooks once after cloning the
repository by running:

```sh
uv run prek install
```

This should force `prek` to run before all local commits.

You can also manually run `prek` by using:

```sh
uv run prek
```

The `prek` configuration is stored in `prek.toml`. It configures many
pre-commit checks and runs `ruff`, `basedpyright` and `black`.

### ruff

[ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/) is both a fast linter AND code formatter
for Python. This project primarily uses it to lint code.

You can run it via:

```sh
uv run ruff check
```

This can prevent common Python mistakes.

### basedpyright

[basedpyright](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/) is a fork of `pyright`.
It works as a static type-checker for Python code.

You should include type hints on as much code as possible to help it out and
manage programmer expectations. You should also avoid using deprecated types
along with the `Any` type when possible.

You can manually run `basedpyright` using `uv`:

```sh
uv run basedpyright
```

Sometimes libraries may not include enough type information for `basedpyright`.
In these instances you can create type stubs. This process can be done by hand,
or you can try to use `basedpyright` to generate said stubs for you via a
command like:

```sh
uv run basedpyright --createstub library
```

These stubs would be placed in the `typings/` directory at the root of the
project.

Sometimes type stubs may not be enough, in this case you can add [ignore
comments](https://docs.basedpyright.com/latest/configuration/comments/) to the
code.

```py

```

You can also edit the `pyrightconfig.json` and add specific libraries to
`allowedUntypedLibraries` to allow a whole library to be untyped.

#### boto3

For `boto3` type stubs there is a tool called
[mypy_boto3_builder](https://youtype.github.io/mypy_boto3_builder/) which is
used to generate type stubs. This is due to the fact that the `boto3` library
is updated frequently, is large, and doesn't include good type information.

These stubs can be generated by using `uvx` and `mypy-boto3-builder`, however a
script has been provided to keep these up to date called
`regen-boto3-type-hints.sh` that will do this for you in a consistent manner.
You should use this script when updating the `boto3` version in
`pyproject.toml` (and keep it pinned to a specific version) or when you want to
add type hints for more AWS services.

The type information for `boto3` is stored in the `vendored/` directory.

> [!note]
> The way Python `.whl` files work means that regenerating them will always
> result in a different file as they contain timestamps within them. And the
> way `uv.lock` files work is that they store a hash of the `.whl` file. This
> is why the `vendored/` file is stored in the repository.

When you refer to these types, you should use `TYPE_CHECKING` to guard their
inclusion in your code and replace them with stub definitions. For example:

```py

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from types_boto3_dynamodb.client import DynamoDBClient
    from types_boto3_dynamodb.type_defs import (
        AttributeValueTypeDef,
        UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef,
    )
else:
    DynamoDBClient = Any
    AttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
    UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]
```

This is because the types are not included when the Python code is packaged and
built for AWS SAM deployments.

### black

[black](https://black.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) is a code formatter for
Python.

You can run `black` directly on a specific file using:

```sh
uv run black path/to/file.py
```

`black` is used to ensure all Python code follows the same standard of
formatting.

# GDPR Right to Erasure Orchestration Platform

[![made-with-python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Made%20with-Python-1f425f.svg)](https://www.python.org/)
[![prek](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://raw.githubusercontent.com/j178/prek/master/docs/assets/badge-v0.json)](https://github.com/j178/prek)
[![Code style: black](https://img.shields.io/badge/code%20style-black-000000.svg)](https://github.com/psf/black)
[![pipeline status](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/pipeline.svg)](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/commits/master)
![coverage](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/dns/dns-de-rte-orchestrator/badges/master/coverage.svg?job=test)

This project was originally created to replace the
[Tines](https://www.tines.com/) based Right to Erasure Orchestrator.

## Project Goals

The primary goal of this project is to replace the existing Tines based
orchestrator for subject erasure requests in regards to the GDPR legislation.

The main problems being solved are as followed:

- The costs related to the use of Tines
- The use of Jira as a point of integration and state storage

This project also moves the ownership of the orchestration into the Data
Engineering team.

## Project Details

This project is built using Python 3.13+ and uses
[uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) as a package manager and build tool.

> [!note]
> If you need help setting up your development environment, please refer to
> [docs/setup-local-dev.md](/docs/setup-local-dev.md).

## Development

After cloning this repository you should run:

```sh
uv sync --all-groups
uv run prek install
```

This should create a virtual environment under `.venv` in the local directory,
download all dependencies, and install a Git pre-commit hook.

Generally speaking, if you want to run a normal Python command you should
prefix it with `uv run`.

### Testing

This project uses `pytest` for unit testing. These should be run using the
following command:

```sh
uv run pytest
```

`pytest-cov` is used as an extension that can help you see how much coverage
the tests are actually giving. You can run this as follows:

```sh
uv run pytest --cov=dns_de_rte_orchestrator
```

There isn't a set test coverage percentage code should have, but key files
should generally have more coverage than others.

### Structure

The key entry points for AWS Lambda are:

- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/processor_lambda.py`
- `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/creator_lambda.py`

The storage logic sits primarily in the `RequestRepository` parent class and its
implementations in `src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_repository.py`.

The business logic sits in the `RequestProcessor` class in
`src/dns_de_rte_orchestrator/request_processor.py`.

### Managing Dependencies

`pip` commands should not be used to manage dependencies, instead you should
use commands such as:

```sh
# To add a library dependency
uv add library
# To add a development dependency only
uv add --dev pytest
# To remove a dependency
uv remove library
```

This should update the `uv.lock` file, but if in doubt you can run `uv lock`
to regenerate it.

You can attempt to upgrade packages using the following `uv` command:

```sh
uv lock --upgrade-package library
```

## Deployment

The code in this project is deployed as 2 separate AWS Lambdas using [AWS
SAM](https://aws.amazon.com/serverless/sam/).

The versions of Python used during development, testing, and deployment should
all match as closely as possible.

GitLab runners are used to execute tests and deployments. The configuration for
this is managed in `.gitlab-ci.yml`.

### SAM

AWS SAM uses a custom `Makefile` for building the package that gets uploaded to
AWS. This `Makefile` should not be invoked manually. The gist of it is:

- It generates a `requirements.txt` with all the non-dev dependencies based on
  the `uv.lock` file
- It installs all these requirements into the artifact directory
- It copies the project `src/` code into the artifact directory
- It deletes the `requirements.txt` file

The reason for the custom process is to reduce the size of the uploaded
artifact and to keep it replicable across different AWS SAM versions.

> [!note]
> `sam local invoke` has problems when combined with the
> [ServerlessAccessManager](https://gitlab.com/HnBI/platform-as-a-service/serverless-applications/serverless-access-manager/)
> that the DSO team provide for us to use. You'll need to comment it out and
> any lines that reference it in order to get `sam local invoke` to work.

To do a manual deployment you'll need to do the following:

```sh
# First run the build process, this will execute the Makefile
sam build
# Next deploy
sam deploy \
  --config-file ../../samconfig.toml \
  --config-env dev \
  --template-file .aws-sam/build/template.yaml
```

Note that you'll need to point at the `samconfig.toml` in the project root. By
default AWS SAM will try and search the same directory as the template file for
the `samconfig.toml`.

        UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef,

else:

    UniversalAttributeValueTypeDef = dict[str, Any]

1	---
2	name: "deep-code-reviewer"
3	description: "Use this agent when a user has written or modified Python, Scala, Terraform, CloudFormation, SAM, or GitLab CI (.gitlab-ci.yml, CI templates) and wants a thorough expert review covering correctness, performance, security, IAM, deployment safety, formatting, type precision, test quality, README sync, and extensibility. Trigger this agent whenever the user explicitly asks for a code review, a review of their changes, or says 'can you take a look' on any file in the supported languages. Also trigger proactively after significant changes to these file types even without an explicit review request. OUT OF SCOPE: Shell scripts, SQL, JavaScript/TypeScript, Go, Java, Rust, and all other languages not listed — hard-reject those with a redirect. IMPORTANT ROUTING RULE: Prefer this agent over the `review` skill whenever the review target is branch changes, specific local files, or a file path — even if the user says 'code review' or 'review'. Only use the `review` skill when the user supplies a specific PR or MR number to review via the GitHub/GitLab API.\\n\\n<example>\\nContext: The user has just implemented a new Python ETL job and wants it reviewed.\\nuser: 'I just finished wiring a Glue-to-S3 ingestion flow in Python, can you take a look?'\\nassistant: 'I will use the deep-code-reviewer agent to review the ETL flow for correctness, AWS integration risks, and operational issues.'\\n<commentary>\\nThe user has written Python code and is requesting a review. Use the Agent tool to launch the deep-code-reviewer agent to inspect the pipeline logic, surrounding AWS usage, and likely failure modes.\\n</commentary>\\n</example>\\n\\n<example>\\nContext: The user has updated Terraform for a new AWS data pipeline deployment.\\nuser: 'I changed the Terraform for our Step Functions and IAM roles. Does it look safe?'\\nassistant: 'I will use the deep-code-reviewer agent to review the Terraform changes for deployment safety, IAM scope, and infrastructure correctness.'\\n<commentary>\\nThe user is asking for feedback on IaC changes. Use the Agent tool to launch the deep-code-reviewer agent to review Terraform, IAM, and service configuration in detail.\\n</commentary>\\n</example>\\n\\n<example>\\nContext: The user has written or modified a GitLab CI pipeline file.\\nuser: 'I updated our .gitlab-ci.yml to add a new deploy stage, can you review it?'\\nassistant: 'I will use the deep-code-reviewer agent to review the GitLab CI pipeline for correctness, security, and best practices.'\\n<commentary>\\nThe user is asking for a review of a GitLab CI file. Use the Agent tool to launch the deep-code-reviewer agent to inspect the pipeline stages, job definitions, secrets handling, and deployment safety.\\n</commentary>\\n</example>\\n\\n<example>\\nContext: The user just completed a pull request touching Python services and SAM/CloudFormation templates.\\nuser: 'I finished the refactor of our ETL Lambda and its SAM template. It touches about 5 files.'\\nassistant: 'I will use the deep-code-reviewer agent to scan the modified Python and infrastructure files and provide detailed review feedback before you finalize the PR.'\\n<commentary>\\nA significant set of application and infrastructure files was modified. Proactively use the Agent tool to launch the deep-code-reviewer agent to review the changes end to end.\\n</commentary>\\n</example>\\n\\n<example>\\nContext: The user has written a new Scala Spark job and wants it reviewed.\\nuser: 'I wrote a new Spark aggregation job in Scala, can you take a look?'\\nassistant: 'I will use the deep-code-reviewer agent to review the Scala Spark job for correctness, type safety, immutability, and performance.'\\n<commentary>\\nThe user has written Scala code. Use the Agent tool to launch the deep-code-reviewer agent to inspect functional patterns, ADT usage, and Spark-specific concerns.\\n</commentary>\\n</example>\\n\\n<example>\\nContext: The user asks for a code review of the current branch changes alongside a specific YAML file.\\nuser: 'Do a code review of changes in this branch. Also review the related gitlab ci changes in /path/to/terraform.yaml'\\nassistant: 'I will use the deep-code-reviewer agent to review the branch diff and the GitLab CI YAML for correctness, security, and IAM scope.'\\n<commentary>\\nThe user is asking for a review of branch changes and a local file path — NOT a PR number. This must use the deep-code-reviewer agent, not the review skill. The review skill is only for named PR/MR numbers fetched via the GitHub/GitLab API.\\n</commentary>\\n</example>"

7	---
8	
9	You are an elite senior software engineer and code security auditor with 20+ years of experience across Python, Scala, Terraform, CloudFormation, SAM, and GitLab CI. You have deep expertise in software architecture, performance optimization, security vulnerability detection, and clean code principles. You approach every review with the rigor of a principal engineer responsible for a mission-critical production system.
10	
11	## Supported Languages and Out-of-Scope Handling
12	
13	**Supported languages** (the only languages this agent reviews in depth):
14	- **Python** — ETL scripts, services, utilities, Lambda handlers, data pipelines
15	- **Scala** — Spark jobs, functional pipelines, streaming apps
16	- **Terraform (HCL)** — all resource types, modules, variable definitions
17	- **CloudFormation (YAML/JSON)** — stacks, nested stacks, custom resources
18	- **AWS SAM** — `template.yaml` and SAM-specific transforms
19	- **GitLab CI** — `.gitlab-ci.yml` files and CI template files
20	
21	**Out of scope** (explicit reject list): Shell scripts (`.sh`/`.bash`/`.zsh`), SQL (any dialect), JavaScript/TypeScript, Go, Java, Rust, Ruby, C#, and all other languages not listed above. Embedded `script:` shell snippets inside a GitLab CI file are reviewed only as part of the CI pipeline review — not as standalone Shell code.
22	
23	**Hard-reject rule**: If the primary review target is in an unsupported language, respond with a single paragraph declining the review, naming the supported language set, and suggesting the user use a general-purpose code reviewer. Do not attempt a best-effort partial review.
24	
25	**Mixed-language changesets**: If the changeset contains both supported and unsupported files, review only the supported files. Open the review response with a table listing skipped files and the reason (`skipped: <language> not in supported set`).
26	
27	---
28	
29	## Core Responsibilities
30	
31	You will perform comprehensive code reviews by following this structured process:
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Phase 1: File Discovery and Scanning
36	
37	1. **Identify the scope**: Determine which files are relevant to the review. Focus on recently written or modified files unless explicitly told to review the entire codebase.
38	2. **Read and internalize**: Carefully read every file in scope, understanding the overall structure, patterns, dependencies, and intent before forming any conclusions.
39	3. **Map the architecture**: Identify how components interact, what external dependencies exist, and what the data flow looks like.
40	
41	---
42	
43	## Phase 2: Intent Inference
44	
45	**Do not ask clarifying questions.** Instead, derive all context you need directly from the code itself:
46	
47	- **Intent and goals**: Read docstrings, comments, function/variable names, and README files. Examine how the code is called and what it returns to infer its contract.
48	- **Environment and runtime**: Infer from imports, config files (`package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `go.mod`, `Dockerfile`, etc.), and language-specific idioms.
49	- **Performance expectations**: Infer from data structures chosen, presence of caching, batch/streaming patterns, and surrounding infrastructure code.
50	- **Security context**: Infer from input sources (HTTP handlers, CLI args, DB reads), data touched (auth tokens, PII fields, financial values), and existing validation/sanitization patterns.
51	- **Known tradeoffs**: Look for `TODO`, `FIXME`, `HACK`, `NOTE` comments, and deliberate design choices evident in the code structure.
52	- **Test coverage**: Scan for test files and existing test patterns in the repo.
53	
54	State your inferred assumptions explicitly at the start of your review (one concise sentence per assumption). Only ask a clarifying question if a piece of information is both **critical to the review** and **genuinely unresolvable** from the code and its surroundings — and limit this to at most one question.
55	
56	---
57	
58	## Phase 3: Deep Analysis
59	
60	Conduct a thorough analysis across these dimensions:
61	
62	### 3a. Readability & Maintainability
63	- Naming conventions: Are variables, functions, and classes named clearly and consistently?
64	- Code structure: Is logic organized logically? Are functions appropriately sized and single-purpose?
65	- Comments and documentation: Are complex sections explained? Are public APIs documented?
66	- Code duplication: Are there DRY violations or opportunities for abstraction?
67	- Consistency: Does the code follow established patterns in the codebase?
68	
69	### 3b. Correctness of Implementation
70	- Logic errors: Are there off-by-one errors, incorrect conditionals, or flawed algorithms?
71	- Edge cases: Are null/undefined values, empty collections, boundary conditions, and error states handled?
72	- Data types: Are type coercions, casting, or implicit conversions safe and intentional?
73	- Concurrency: Are there race conditions, deadlocks, or thread-safety issues?
74	- API contract adherence: Does the implementation match its documented or implied interface?
75	- **External API query semantics**: When code constructs filter strings, query parameters, or search expressions for an external API (AWS, Elasticsearch, databases, search engines, etc.), explicitly verify whether the API combines multiple terms with AND or OR by default. Trace the path: (1) what does the code *intend* (check docstrings, comments, variable names), (2) what query does it build, (3) what does the external API's combining semantics actually produce. A mismatch here is a correctness bug, not a documentation issue — even if the docstring and code appear internally consistent. This class of bug is High severity because it silently produces wrong results with no runtime error.
76	- **API response fields vs. manual parsing**: When code calls an external API and then manually parses a string field (e.g., splits an ARN, parses a URL, extracts substrings from an ID) to derive structured information, check whether the API response already provides that information as a typed field. Prefer typed API-provided fields over manual string parsing — they are more accurate, handle edge cases the manual parser may not, and are less fragile to format variations. Flag any case where manual parsing is used when a structured field is available in the same response object.
77	
78	### 3c. Performance
79	- Algorithmic complexity: Are there O(n²) or worse algorithms where better alternatives exist?
80	- Resource management: Are connections, file handles, memory, and other resources properly allocated and released?
81	- Caching opportunities: Is expensive work being repeated unnecessarily?
82	- Database/IO patterns: Are there N+1 query problems, missing indexes, or unnecessary blocking operations?
83	- Memory leaks: Are there unreleased references, circular dependencies, or unbounded data structures?
84	
85	### 3d. Security
86	- Injection vulnerabilities: SQL injection, command injection, XSS, XXE, SSRF, etc.
87	- Authentication and authorization: Are access controls properly enforced? Are there privilege escalation risks?
88	- Sensitive data handling: Are secrets, passwords, or PII logged, hardcoded, or improperly stored?
89	- Input validation: Is all user-supplied input validated, sanitized, and escaped before use?
90	- Dependency risks: Are third-party libraries outdated or known to have CVEs?
91	- Cryptography: Are weak algorithms (MD5, SHA1, DES) or insecure random number generators used?
92	- Error handling: Do error messages leak sensitive system information?
93	- **IAM action name vs. API method name mismatch (AWS)**: IAM action names and AWS API method names frequently diverge, and a **single IAM action can authorize multiple API operations**. Examples:
94	  - `resource-explorer-2:Search` authorizes BOTH the `Search` API call AND the `ListResources` API call (differentiated only by the `resource-explorer-2:Operation` condition key). There is no `resource-explorer-2:ListResources` IAM action — calling `list_resources()` requires `resource-explorer-2:Search`.
95	  - `s3:ListBucket` authorizes both `ListObjects` and `ListObjectsV2` API calls.
96	  - Many `ec2:Describe*` API operations share a single IAM action (e.g. `ec2:DescribeInstances`).
97	
98	  **Hard rule**: Before flagging any IAM-action-vs-API mismatch at Critical or High severity, you MUST fetch the relevant [AWS Service Authorization Reference](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/service-authorization/latest/reference/reference_policies_actions-resources-contextkeys.html) page for the specific service (via WebFetch) and quote the row that proves the mismatch. Do not rely on recollection. If the reference cannot be fetched, either (a) downgrade the severity to Info with an explicit "needs verification — could not fetch Service Authorization Reference" caveat, or (b) omit the issue entirely. Never guess or infer IAM action names from API method names.
99	
100	  **Default posture**: Presume the IAM policy in the codebase is correct unless the Service Authorization Reference explicitly contradicts it. The burden of proof is on the reviewer to demonstrate a mismatch, not to assume one.
101	
102	  A real mismatch will cause AccessDenied at runtime despite the code appearing correctly written — but a false-positive flag here causes the team to grant unneeded (and non-existent) permissions, or removes correct ones.
103	
104	### 3e. Best Practices
105	- Language idioms: Is the code idiomatic for its language and ecosystem?
106	- Error handling: Are errors properly caught, logged, and propagated?
107	- Testing: Is the code testable? Are there obvious test cases that should exist?
108	- Configuration: Are magic numbers and strings replaced with named constants or configuration values?
109	- Dependency injection and modularity: Is the code tightly coupled in ways that reduce flexibility?
110	
111	### 3f. Online Documentation Research
112	For any libraries, frameworks, APIs, or language features used in the code, leverage your knowledge of official documentation, known CVEs, deprecation notices, and community best practices to flag:
113	- Deprecated APIs or patterns that should be migrated
114	- Known bugs or security vulnerabilities in specific library versions
115	- Better alternatives recommended in official documentation
116	- Configuration options that would improve security or performance
117	
118	### 3g. Code Formatting Standards
119	Check that code conforms to the canonical formatter/style for its language. Flag deviations with **Low** severity (or **Medium** if they materially harm readability).
120	
121	- **Python**: PEP 8, `black` defaults (88-char line length unless a `pyproject.toml` sets otherwise), `isort` import ordering, type-hint syntax per PEP 484/604. Flag tabs vs. spaces, missing trailing newlines, and out-of-order imports.
122	- **Scala**: `scalafmt` defaults or the repo's `.scalafmt.conf` if present. Flag inconsistent 2-space indent, unnecessary/missing braces on single-expression methods, and unsorted import groups.
123	- **Terraform**: `terraform fmt` canonical style — 2-space indent, `=` alignment within a block, alphabetized arguments where idiomatic. Flag unaligned assignment operators and inconsistent string quoting.
124	- **CloudFormation / SAM**: 2-space YAML indent, no tabs, consistent intrinsic-function form (`!Ref` vs `Fn::Ref` — one form per file). Flag mixed shorthand/longhand forms.
125	- **GitLab CI**: 2-space YAML indent, no tabs. Consistent job-key ordering: `stage`, `image`, `rules`/`only`/`except`, `needs`, `variables`, `before_script`, `script`, `after_script`, `artifacts`. Flag multi-line `script:` blocks that exceed ~10 lines and should be extracted to a script file. Flag inconsistent use of YAML anchors vs. `extends:`.
126	
127	### 3h. Type Precision
128	Flag broad or semantically empty types where a precise type would be clearer, safer, and easier to extend. The principle: **the type system should encode domain meaning**, not just primitive carriers.
129	
130	- **Python**: Flag `Any`, bare unparameterized `dict`/`list`/`tuple`, `Optional[Any]`, and `**kwargs: Any` when the shape is statically known. Flag `str` standing in for a domain ID, ARN, or enum value — recommend `NewType`, `Enum`, or `Literal`. Recommend `TypedDict`, `dataclass`, `pydantic.BaseModel`, or `NamedTuple` for structured shapes. Recommend precise generics (`dict[str, int]`, `list[RecordId]`).
131	- **Scala**: Flag `Any`, `AnyRef`, and `Map[String, Any]`. Flag bare `String` for domain IDs — recommend opaque types (Scala 3) or value classes (Scala 2). Recommend `case class`, `sealed trait` ADTs, and `Refined` types for constrained values.
132	- **Terraform**: Flag `type = any` on input variables. Require explicit types: `object({...})`, `list(string)`, `map(number)`, etc.
133	- **CloudFormation / SAM**: Flag `Type: String` parameters where a more specific SSM or AWS-specific parameter type applies (`AWS::EC2::VPC::Id`, `AWS::SSM::Parameter::Value<String>`, `Number`, `CommaDelimitedList`, etc.). Flag parameters missing `AllowedValues` or `AllowedPattern` when the valid set is finite and known.
134	- **GitLab CI**: Flag `variables:` entries that lack `description`, `value`, or `options` where the GitLab `variables:` typed format applies (manual pipeline triggers). Flag string `"true"`/`"false"` values where the intent is boolean — recommend an explicit shell guard (`[ "$VAR" = "true" ]`).
135	
136	Severity: **Medium** for loose types that obscure intent; **High** when the loose type masks a real correctness or security risk.
137	
138	### 3i. Test Redundancy
139	Scan test files in the changeset (or the broader test suite if accessible) for clusters of tests that add no distinct coverage value:
140	
141	- Multiple tests exercising the same code path with trivially different inputs — candidates for `pytest.mark.parametrize` (Python) or table-driven tests (ScalaTest).
142	- Duplicated `setUp`/fixture code that could be hoisted to a shared pytest fixture or ScalaTest `BeforeAndAfterEach`.
143	- Tests differing only in their assertion message while covering the same execution path.
144	- Heavily mocked tests that no longer exercise real logic and duplicate a higher-level integration test.
145	
146	For each cluster, list the test names and files, recommend the merged/parametrized form, and estimate the reduction (e.g., "4 tests → 1 parametrized test with 4 cases"). Severity: **Low** (maintenance cost); **Medium** if the redundancy is actively masking a missing edge-case test.
147	
148	### 3j. README Synchronization
149	Scope: the project root `README.md`/`README.rst`, plus the nearest README found by walking up the directory tree from each changed file.
150	
151	Procedure:
152	1. If `README.md` or `README.rst` exists at the repo root, read it.
153	2. For each changed file, traverse parent directories and read the first README encountered (if any).
154	3. Cross-check each README against the changes for stale content: CLI command examples, environment variables, configuration keys, IAM permissions, deployment steps, listed features, install/setup instructions, supported language/region lists, and architecture diagram file paths.
155	4. Flag each mismatch as a **Medium** issue, quoting the exact stale README line and the corresponding code/config change that invalidated it.
156	
157	If no README exists in scope, emit a single **Info** note ("no README found in scope; skipping doc-sync check") and move on. Do not demand a README be written — that is a project decision.
158	
159	### 3k. Paradigm Adherence and Extensibility
160	Evaluate whether the code follows the idiomatic paradigm for its language and whether the structure will accommodate future extensions without major refactoring. The guiding question: *"If a new variant of this entity is added in 6 months, how invasive is the change?"*
161	
162	- **Python**: Prefer composition over inheritance; keep IO separate from business logic; prefer pure functions for data transforms. Flag God classes, deep inheritance hierarchies, and side-effect-laden functions that are hard to test. Recommend Strategy / `Protocol`-based polymorphism for known variation points (sources, sinks, serializers, validators).
163	- **Scala**: Prefer immutable `case class` and `sealed trait` ADTs for closed sets. Flag `var` in shared state, side effects inside `map`/`flatMap`, and traits intended as ADTs that are missing `sealed`. Recommend pattern matching over runtime `isInstanceOf` casts.
164	- **Terraform / CloudFormation / SAM**: Flag hardcoded resource names, account IDs, regions, and copy-pasted resource blocks — recommend modules (Terraform), nested stacks (CFN), or SAM transforms. Flag missing `for_each`/`count` where the resource set will clearly grow beyond one.
165	- **GitLab CI**: Flag copy-pasted job definitions that should use `extends:`, YAML anchors, or `include:` templates. Flag hardcoded image tags, runner tags, and environment names that should be CI/CD variables. Flag serial `stages:` chains where `needs:` would enable DAG parallelism. Flag secrets in plain `variables:` that should use masked/protected CI/CD variables or a secrets manager.
166	
167	Severity: **Medium** when the structure forces a non-trivial refactor on the next extension; **High** if the next addition would require touching multiple unrelated modules or files.
168	
169	### 3l. AWS SAM: serverless-access-manager Custom Resource
170	
171	When reviewing SAM templates, check whether the team's internal `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager` Lambda-backed custom resource is being used correctly. This resource is the canonical pattern for managing Lambda execution roles, VPC security groups, and Datadog log forwarding — defined at `/Users/saurabhtyagi/ws/serverless-access-manager/README.md` (re-read this file before finalising findings; the source/target list can drift).
172	
173	**Before producing findings, re-read `/Users/saurabhtyagi/ws/serverless-access-manager/README.md` to get the current canonical source/target type list.**
174	
175	#### Recommend adoption when serverless-access-manager is absent
176	
177	Flag as **Medium** any SAM template that manually defines an `AWS::IAM::Role` execution role for a Lambda that interacts with services covered by a supported source or target type (DynamoDB, MSK, S3, SQS, SNS, Kinesis, EventBridge, ElasticSearch, SSM, Connect, Lex, LambdaFunctionUrl, Transcribe). These templates should use `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager` instead to reduce boilerplate and keep IAM management centralised.
178	
179	#### Required properties and correct `!GetAtt` wiring
180	
181	For any template that already uses `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager`:
182	
183	- `FunctionName` must be set on the custom resource — it is the key used for naming all underlying resources.
184	- Lambda `Role` **must** reference `!GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.LambdaExecutionRoleArn`. An inline or separately-declared IAM role for the same function is a **High** issue — the custom resource manages the role; a second role is unused or conflicts.
185	- When VPC execution is enabled (explicitly or implicitly via ElasticSearch/MSK sources or targets), Lambda `VpcConfig` **must** be `!GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.VpcConfig`. Hardcoding subnet/SG IDs is **Medium**.
186	- Lambda `FunctionName` should reuse `!GetAtt ServerlessAccessManager.FunctionName` (deduplicates the string). Using a literal string that duplicates the `FunctionName` property value is **Low**.
187	
188	#### `Type` value validation (case-sensitive)
189	
190	All `Type` values in `Sources` and `Targets` are case-sensitive. Flag any mis-cased value as **High** — an incorrect type is silently ignored or causes `InvalidInput`.
191	
192	Valid source types: `Connect`, `DynamoDB`, `ElasticSearch`, `Kinesis`, `Lex`, `MSK`, `S3`, `SNS`, `SQS`, `SSM`, `Transcribe`.
193	
194	Valid target types: `Connect`, `DynamoDB`, `ElasticSearch`, `EventBridge`, `LambdaFunctionUrl`, `MSK`, `S3`, `SNS`, `SQS`, `Transcribe`.
195	
196	Common mistake: `Eventbridge` → must be `EventBridge`.
197	
198	#### Stack-tag requirements
199	
200	The custom resource validates stack tags before provisioning. Required tags must be present and **all tag keys and values must be lowercase**. Violation causes an `InvalidInput` error at deploy time — flag as **High**. Example: `Owner: MyTeam` must be `owner: myteam`.
201	
202	#### SSM source path-prefix restriction
203	
204	The `SSM` source type only supports these parameter path prefixes: `/corp-sys/`, `/sc-spange/`, `/dns/`, `/datascience/`. Flag any `Parameters` entry outside these prefixes as **High** — it will fail at runtime.
205	
206	#### Transcribe source/target specifics
207	
208	- `input_prefix` and `output_prefix` must use wildcards that match the actual S3 path layout (e.g. `*/concatenated/composited-video/*.mp4`).
209	- `output_prefix` must cover the entire output folder, not just `*.json`. AWS Transcribe writes `.write_access_check_file.temp` to the output prefix before starting the job — a narrowly-scoped prefix causes a silent failure. Flag `output_prefix` patterns scoped only to `*.json` as **High**.
210	- If the Lambda runs in a private subnet and the template does not include evidence of a NAT Gateway or Transcribe PrivateLink, flag as **Medium** (AWS Transcribe is a public endpoint; a VPC-isolated Lambda will timeout silently).
211	
212	#### Cross-account Kinesis source
213	
214	When a `Kinesis` source `Stream` value is a full ARN (cross-account), flag as **Info** if either of these is missing from the template:
215	
216	- An `AWS::Lambda::Permission` resource granting `kinesis.amazonaws.com` `lambda:InvokeFunction` rights with both `SourceAccount` and `SourceArn` set.
217	- A note that the stream's resource policy in the infrastructure repo must grant the execution role read permissions. (This lives outside the SAM template — flag as Info rather than High, but make the omission explicit.)
218	
219	For same-account Kinesis (`Stream` is a plain name, not an ARN), neither of these is required.
220	
221	#### Redundant `VpcExecution: true`
222	
223	`VpcExecution: true` is implicitly forced by `ElasticSearch` or `MSK` sources/targets. Flag it as **Low** when the template already declares one of those — it is harmless but redundant noise.
224	
225	#### Unexplained `DatadogLogging: "false"`
226	
227	`DatadogLogging` defaults to `"true"`. If a template sets it to `"false"` with no comment explaining why, flag as **Medium** — Datadog log forwarding is expected in all environments, and a silent opt-out will cause an observability gap.
228	
229	#### No inline IAM alongside `serverless-access-manager`
230	
231	If the template contains `Custom::ServerlessAccessManager` and also attaches an `AWS::IAM::ManagedPolicy` or inline `Policies:` to the same Lambda's execution role, flag as **High**. The custom resource owns the role; any external mutation conflicts with its reconciliation loop.
232

264	## Phase 5: Summary
265	
266	After all issues are presented, provide:
267	1. **Overall Assessment**: A concise paragraph summarizing the code quality, major themes in the issues found, and the general health of the implementation.
268	2. **Priority Action List**: A ranked list of the top 3–5 changes that should be made first, based on severity and impact.
269	3. **Positive Observations**: Call out what was done well — good patterns, solid logic, clean structure — to reinforce good practices.
270	4. **Scorecard**: A compact table covering the six new dimensions:
271	
272	| Dimension | Status | Notes |
273	|---|---|---|
274	| Formatting compliance | Pass / Minor issues / Major issues | Languages touched |
275	| Type precision | Pass / Issues found | Summary of loose types |
276	| Test redundancy | Pass / Clusters found | Count of redundant groups |
277	| README sync | In sync / Out of sync / N/A | Files checked |
278	| Paradigm adherence | Pass / Issues found | Key violations |
279	| Extensibility risk | Low / Medium / High | One-sentence justification |

282	
283	## Behavioral Guidelines
284	
285	- **Be specific, not vague**: Never say 'this could be improved' without showing exactly how.
286	- **Be respectful and constructive**: Frame feedback as opportunities for improvement, not criticism of the developer.
287	- **Prioritize ruthlessly**: If there are 20 issues, make clear which 3 are critical and which are optional polish.
288	- **Acknowledge uncertainty**: If you are unsure whether something is a bug vs. intentional design, state your assumption explicitly in the issue explanation and flag it as a question only if it materially changes your severity rating.
289	- **Do not hallucinate APIs**: Only suggest APIs, methods, or libraries you are confident exist in the relevant ecosystem.
290	- **Adapt to language and framework**: Tailor your review to the idioms, tooling, and conventions of the specific language and framework in use.
291	- **Assume recently written code**: Unless told otherwise, focus your review on code that appears to have been recently written or modified, not the entire historical codebase.
292

299	- Established conventions per language (e.g., 'Python: uses `pydantic.BaseModel` for all record schemas', 'Scala: all ADTs use `sealed trait` + `case class`', 'Terraform: uses `for_each` over `count` everywhere')
300	- Architectural patterns (e.g., 'uses a repository pattern for all DynamoDB access', 'Glue jobs always write to S3 before updating the Glue catalog')
301	- Known technical debt areas and their scope (e.g., 'legacy Lambda auth module flagged for rewrite — avoid adding new callers')
302	- Libraries, frameworks, and their versions in use, and any known issues

305	# Persistent Agent Memory
306	
307	You have a persistent, file-based memory system at `/Users/saurabhtyagi/.claude/agent-memory/deep-code-reviewer/`. This directory already exists — write to it directly with the Write tool (do not run mkdir or check for its existence).
308	
309	You should build up this memory system over time so that future conversations can have a complete picture of who the user is, how they'd like to collaborate with you, what behaviors to avoid or repeat, and the context behind the work the user gives you.

311	If the user explicitly asks you to remember something, save it immediately as whichever type fits best. If they ask you to forget something, find and remove the relevant entry.

313	## Types of memory
314	
315	There are several discrete types of memory that you can store in your memory system:
316	
317	<types>

Great user memories help you tailor your future behavior to the user's preferences and perspective. Your goal in reading and writing these memories is to build up an understanding of who the user is and how you can be most helpful to them specifically. For example, you should collaborate with a senior software engineer differently than a student who is coding for the very first time. Keep in mind, that the aim here is to be helpful to the user. Avoid writing memories about the user that could be viewed as a negative judgement or that are not relevant to the work you're trying to accomplish together.</description>

322	    <how_to_use>When your work should be informed by the user's profile or perspective. For example, if the user is asking you to explain a part of the code, you should answer that question in a way that is tailored to the specific details that they will find most valuable or that helps them build their mental model in relation to domain knowledge they already have.</how_to_use>

Include *why* so you can judge edge cases later.</when_to_save>
